primary_diagnosis.name: Reflex (neurocardiogenic) faint provoked by a blood/injury/medical-procedure visual trigger — first uncomplicated episode with a mechanical fall

pathophysiology_summary: A strong emotional/visual stimulus (the sight of a surgical procedure) in a warm environment triggered an exaggerated autonomic reflex: the Bezold-Jarisch-type response produces abrupt withdrawal of sympathetic tone with a surge of parasympathetic (vagal) output, causing peripheral vasodilatation and bradycardia. Cerebral perfusion falls, producing the classic autonomic prodrome (nausea, diaphoresis, warmth, tunnel vision, tinnitus) and then transient global cerebral hypoperfusion with brief loss of consciousness and loss of postural tone. Assuming a supine posture restores venous return and cerebral perfusion, giving the characteristically rapid, complete recovery without a post-ictal state. The history is textbook for a benign reflex faint; however, the diagnosis is clinical and one of exclusion in a first event — cardiac (arrhythmic/structural) syncope, seizure and orthostatic causes must be considered, and a fall with impaired consciousness carries an occult head-injury risk. None of the confirmatory in-person findings (normal neurological exam, dual heart sounds with no murmur, normal orthostatic vitals, a 12-lead ECG) can be obtained over telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Emotional/visual trigger, warm room, seated position and a transient loss of consciousness in a young adult.
Any transient loss of consciousness may be cardiac; must be excluded before attributing to a reflex faint, especially in a first event.
Sudden collapse with loss of consciousness raises seizure; features (aura vs autonomic prodrome, convulsion, tongue-biting, post-ictal confusion) discriminate.
A common cause of transient loss of consciousness, but the patient was seated (not rising) and there was a clear autonomic prodrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, all four should be live. The AI Doctor must NOT close on 'benign faint' before screening cardiac and seizure red flags.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic autonomic prodrome, positional trigger, rapid complete recovery when supine, no exertion, no cardiac symptoms, no convulsive or post-ictal features.
Made less likely by the long vagal prodrome, seated non-exertional onset and absent family history, but CANNOT be excluded without an ECG — hence same-day in-person assessment.
No witnessed convulsion, no tongue-biting, no incontinence and no post-ictal confusion; immediate orientation on recovery.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Cardiac syncope stays on the list as important-not-to-miss even after a reassuring history — the correct disposition is in-person assessment with an ECG, not telehealth reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Reflex (vasovagal) syncope — presumptive, pending in-person assessment and ECG
differential.evidence_basis: Textbook reflex faint; benign in-person examination and a normal ECG would confirm it and exclude a cardiac cause. Telehealth can reach a presumptive diagnosis only.

differential.diagnosis: Occult head injury from the fall
differential.evidence_basis: The patient fell with impaired consciousness and does not recall the fall; a head strike cannot be excluded, so a delayed intracranial bleed must be safety-netted.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Two things must survive to the plan: (1) same-day in-person evaluation with an ECG to exclude cardiac syncope; (2) a head-injury watch escalating to 000.

red_flags.description: Syncope during exertion, or with no prodrome ('drop attack')
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exertional or unheralded syncope strongly suggests a cardiac (arrhythmic or structural) cause requiring urgent cardiology assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed arrhythmic syncope can be the only warning before sudden cardiac death.

red_flags.description: Chest pain or palpitations preceding the collapse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Points toward an arrhythmic or ischaemic cardiac cause rather than a reflex faint.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate a cardiac syncope for ECG/monitoring risks a fatal recurrence.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden cardiac death or inherited arrhythmia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raises the probability of an inherited channelopathy or cardiomyopathy presenting as syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating inherited risk can miss a life-threatening, treatable cause.

red_flags.description: Head strike / possible head injury sustained during the collapse, with amnesia for the fall
red_flags.clinical_significance: Loss of consciousness with a fall carries an occult intracranial-injury risk; delayed bleeding can present hours later.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed delayed intracranial haemorrhage can be fatal if warning signs are not safety-netted.

red_flags.description: Convulsive movements, tongue-biting, incontinence or prolonged post-event confusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their presence would point to a seizure rather than a reflex faint; their absence supports syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling a first seizure as a faint delays neurological work-up and driving/safety advice.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The history is so classic that the reasoner closes immediately and reassures at home, skipping the mandatory exclusion of cardiac syncope in a first event.

Attempting to definitively diagnose a benign reflex faint remotely when the confirmatory findings (normal neuro exam, no murmur, normal orthostatic vitals, a normal ECG) can only be obtained in person.
Focusing on the faint and forgetting that a collapse with impaired consciousness and amnesia for the fall needs an occult-head-injury safety-net.
Reaching for drug therapy (e.g. a beta-blocker or a vasopressor) for an uncomplicated first reflex faint — not indicated; first-line management is education and physical counter-pressure manoeuvres.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure, heart rate and temperature (clinician-measured: BP supine 110/70, HR 68, Temp 36.6 C, RR 14, SpO2 100%)
A focused neurological examination (power, sensation, coordination, nystagmus, focal deficits)
Cardiac auscultation for murmurs and rhythm
A 12-lead ECG to exclude conduction disease, pre-excitation or a long-QT interval
Direct inspection/palpation of the scalp for occult head trauma
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange an urgent SAME-DAY in-person assessment (GP or urgent care) for a first transient loss of consciousness with a fall — to examine the head, check orthostatic vitals and obtain an ECG to exclude a cardiac cause. Provide a head-injury safety-net that escalates to 000 for any delayed-bleed warning signs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High risk of UNDER-action: the classic story invites remote reassurance and discharge, but a first syncope with a fall cannot be cleared of a cardiac cause or occult head injury over chat.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A resting 12-lead ECG is recommended in the initial evaluation of all patients with syncope; reflex syncope is a clinical diagnosis after cardiac causes are considered and excluded.
